title: 社交媒体有什么好处吗？
title_en: Is Social Media Good For Anything At All?

note:
苹果(Apple)和Facebook再次发生争执，你对此的感受表明，在2020年奇怪的科技版图中，你相信谁能代表你的利益。
从一个可能会让你不由自主地目瞪口呆的相当学术性的问题开始：什么时候支持隐私的监管超越了它的界限，变成了反竞争？这是我去年发现自己在问的一个问题，当时苹果公司暂时取消了Facebook的企业证书，因为有爆料称，该公司一直在使用这些证书进行市场调查。这也是我今天在思考的一个问题，因为苹果进行干预，单方面重塑了一个市场--以一种让Facebook再次处于守势的方式。
这是一个技术问题，但它值得了解一下-如果只是因为这样一个事实，假设你使用iPhone，那么一旦你在大约一个月的时间里升级你的设备，你的手机上就会出现很多弹出窗口。广告业为每个设备分配了一个唯一的代码，称为广告商标识，或IDFA。了解你的IDFA可以帮助广告商判断他们的广告是否有效，特别是当他们在多个地方向你展示相同的广告时。Facebook使用IDFA作为其面向开发者的广告网络Audience Network的一部分。
从今年秋天发布的iOS14开始，苹果将开始要求开发者向你显示他们正在收集你的IDFA的警告，你必须选择分享它。预计会有很大一部分用户会说“不用了，谢谢”。
周三，Facebook表示将完全停止收集IDFA。以下是彭博社的库尔特·瓦格纳：
Facebook周三表示，不会在iOS14设备上通过自己的应用程序收集IDFA，这一决定将“严重影响”观众网络。该公司表示，数以千计的开发商使用Facebook平台来填充其移动应用程序中的广告库存，如果没有IDFA信息来帮助定向这些营销信息，受众网络收入可能会下降高达50%。Facebook正在考虑完全取消针对iOS14用户的这项服务。
Facebook在一篇博客文章中写道：“这不是我们想要做出的改变，但不幸的是，苹果对iOS14的更新迫使我们做出了这一决定。”“我们知道这可能会严重影响出版商在iOS 14上通过Audience Network赚钱的能力，尽管我们尽了最大努力，但可能会使Audience Network在iOS 14上变得非常无效，以至于未来在iOS 14上提供它可能没有意义。”
Facebook自己的广告业务大体上会很好，因为你在其应用程序上的个人资料和活动包含的数据比简单的设备ID有用得多。但该公司表示，它的许多广告客户将受到影响。
今天推特上对这一切的普遍反应是：嘘-呼。“哇，可怜的孩子，Facebook，”我的朋友乔安娜·斯特恩(Joanna Stern)在一条具有代表性的推文中说。“现在，它必须征得许可，才能跟踪你在手机上做的每一件事。”这位化名为“屎的互联网”的网友说：“我喜欢Facebook把这件事说成是一件坏事。”
需要明确的是，苹果在这里的举动合法地促进了隐私，即使只是在边际。它这样做的方式与Facebook经常庆祝自己所做的事情的方式相同：让用户掌握控制权。我的朋友沃尔特·莫斯伯格(Walt Mossberg)谈到了这一点：“在即将发布的iOS版本中，苹果只是将那些想要跟踪网络用户的应用程序从一个模糊的选择退出系统转变为一个明确的选择加入系统，”他写道。“终于！在我看来，我们应该制定一项法律，明确规定收集个人资料时必须选择加入。“。
但是，知道苹果使用从你的手机上收集的各种数据来针对你投放自己的广告，你会有什么不同的感觉吗？因为它就是这样！以下是《华尔街日报》上的Patience Haggin和Jeff Horwitz：
苹果在在线广告方面并不是一个大玩家，但它确实有自己的小业务，根据用户去哪里和用户在苹果应用程序中做什么，个性化显示在App Store和Apple News上的广告。该公司正在对自己的广告个性化应用单独的规则；要选择退出，用户必须在iPhone的设置中找到一个选项。
苹果公司表示，它在所有地方都采用同一标准。IDFA限制仅适用于希望在公司之间移动数据的开发人员；所有开发人员都可以访问从他们自己的应用程序中生成的数据。但这巧妙地搁置了这样一个事实，即苹果不必在公司之间转移数据来运营其广告业务-因为它拥有设备、操作系统、应用商店、数据和广告网络。
好的，但是谁才是真正的受害者呢？我们知道Facebook可以承受打击，大型游戏发行商肯定会找到其他方式为他们的游戏做广告。但Recode报道称，大型出版商正在考虑完全放弃iOS，因为预计广告价值将大幅下降：
“这让我们很难将我们的苹果应用程序用户货币化，因为他们是令人难以置信的忠实读者。坦率地说，这会危及我们提供苹果应用的能力，“DMG Media的出版商马丁·克拉克(Martin Clarke)说。DMG Media拥有英国的”每日邮报“(Daily Mail)和其他出版物。该公司表示，其MailOnline iOS应用每天吸引120万用户。“为一个盈利能力不如其他平台的平台提供应用程序是没有意义的。”
如此急剧的下降以前也发生过。据The Information去年报道，当苹果在其Safari网络浏览器上实施类似的反跟踪功能时，接触到那里的客户的成本下降了60%。如果你相信免费的、有广告支持的新闻对健康的民主是有益的，那么值得注意的是，所有这些有利于隐私的改变都是有代价的。
我发誓，我不喜欢广告。我每个月都要付额外的钱在YouTube，Hulu，甚至是Verge上关闭它们。但是，广告补贴了很多重要的服务，这也是事实。你的钱越少，你就越依赖Facebook等公司提供的免费服务来过日常生活。
广告商可以收集的数据种类，他们可以用这些数据做什么，以及他们如何存储这些数据，绝对应该有严格的限制。可以说，对所有这些事情都应该有比今天更多的限制。
但是，就像当我看到苹果对一家电子邮件初创公司的收入模式发号施令，或者威胁说，如果WordPress的母公司不实施应用内购买，就不让WordPress博客所有者修复漏洞一样，当我看到一家企业可以在多大程度上改变整个经济的条款时，我也会感到担忧。特别是在没有任何受影响的人有任何有意义的上诉方式的情况下。
很少有人会为Facebook在IFDA上流泪。我理解许多人有一种冲动，想要通过好的团队击败坏的团队来看待这场特别的争端。但今年夏天的事件让我在硅谷认识的人一遍又一遍地问着同样的问题：苹果下一步会挤压谁？
趋势下降：根据亚利桑那州对科技巨头谷歌提起的消费者欺诈诉讼最新公布的文件，谷歌员工知道公司的位置隐私设置令人困惑，并可能具有误导性。在这起诉讼之前，美联社发表了一篇关于谷歌如何跟踪用户的文章-即使是那些已经关闭跟踪的用户。(凯特·考克斯/Ars Technica)。
⭐周二晚间，威斯康星州基诺沙的两名抗议者被打死。暴力事件可能与一个自称民兵组织在Facebook上发布的活动有关，该组织称这一事件为“战斗号召”。Facebook已经删除了这一活动，以及该组织的Facebook页面。下面是罗素·布兰奇在The Verge的报道：
三天来，基诺沙一直饱受抗议活动的困扰，抗议活动的焦点是29岁的雅各布·布莱克(Jacob Blake)被警方从背后开枪打死，布莱克是六个孩子的父亲。抗议活动造成了重大的财产损失，周一晚间摧毁了当地惩教部的一个设施。
在周二下午的一篇帖子中，基诺沙卫队Facebook小组鼓励对正在进行的骚乱做出武装回应。“今晚有爱国者愿意拿起武器保卫我们的城市不受邪恶暴徒的伤害吗？”帖子上写着。“毫无疑问，他们目前正在计划今晚烧毁这座城市的下一个部分。”
周二晚上11点45分，抗议者与手持长步枪的男子发生冲突，造成两人死亡，第三人受伤。枪击事件的视频似乎显示，一名武装民兵成员向抗议者开枪。当地警长办公室仍在调查这起枪击事件，肇事者尚未确定。
至少有两名不同的Facebook用户报告说，基诺沙卫队的账户在枪击事件发生前煽动暴力。在每种情况下，Facebook的版主都检查了这个群，发现它没有违反平台的政策。枪击事件发生后，该公司关闭了基诺沙警卫队的Facebook页面。(罗素·布兰登/The Verge)。
在Twitter和YouTube用户试图重现枪击事件之际，社交媒体上确认了被控在威斯康星州基诺沙杀害两名抗议者的年轻人的身份。凯尔·里滕豪斯被认定为“逃犯”，将被指控犯有一级故意杀人罪。(Adi Robertson/The Verge)。
推特暂停了一些账号，因为他们分享了一条病毒式信息，自称是一名黑人生命也是抗议者，他之前投票给了民主党人，但现在改投共和党人的票。这条信息首先被一个看似不真实的账户分享，然后被其他人复制和粘贴。(玛丽安娜·斯普林/BBC)。
QAnon阴谋论者在Facebook上瞄准福音派。他们传递的信息是，唐纳德·特朗普(Donald Trump)正在与一个由自由派精英运营的秘密撒旦恋童癖团伙作战，这反映了20世纪80年代和90年代的“撒旦恐慌”。(Abby Ohlheiser/MIT技术评论)。
2400多个警察部门已经与有争议的面部识别公司Clearview AI签署了合同。在对Jason Calacanis的视频采访中，该公司的首席执行官说：“现在能成为辩论的中心并谈论隐私是一种荣誉。”很荣幸！(伊丽莎白·洛帕托/The Verge)。
英国政府可能会通过对TikTok的限制，但不会阻止该公司在伦敦设立国际总部。但这些规定将阻止TikTok将用户数据转移到国外。(Kitty Donaldson，Katharine Gemmell和Nate Lanxon/彭博社)。
一个由2000多名在线卖家组成的团体在印度对亚马逊提起了反垄断诉讼。诉讼称，亚马逊偏袒一些打折销售商品的供应商，并将独立零售商赶走。(Aditya Kalra/路透社)
苏格兰维基百科的主要管理员⭐是一个不认识任何苏格兰人的美国人，他用拼写错误的英语写文章。这种情况突显了该网站的一个主要问题-许多语言版本的维基百科只有几个编辑，这些人对网站上最终出现的内容拥有过大的控制权。小爱德华·翁维索(Edward Ongweso Jr.)。At NEVICE描述了这个问题：
例如，几年前发现克罗地亚维基百科的一名管理员否认大屠杀，并将这些观点插入克罗地亚维基百科的文章中。几乎没有人能阻止他们。由于维基百科在谷歌上的排名如此之高，并已被视为一个值得信赖的、中立的信息来源，因此它经常被机器学习研究人员用作语料库来训练语言，而被普通人用作进入某个主题的第一个切入点。
Reddit的帖子接着说，Amaryllis造成的损害可能比任何人预期的都严重。甚至在这一切还没有开始之前，人们显然对苏格兰语言有一个糟糕的概念，这直接源于其维基百科的状况。因此，“这个人对苏格兰语言的破坏可能比历史上任何人都大。”由于他们的“文化破坏行为”，很多人可能认为苏格兰人是“对英语的可怕破坏”，而不是它自己的语言。
微软计划收购TikTok的一小部分股权，并成为该应用程序的少数投资者之一。后来特朗普总统介入，这一悄无声息的交易变成了一部国际肥皂剧。这个故事记录了它是如何发生的。(迈克·艾萨克和安德鲁·罗斯·索尔金/《纽约时报》)。
软银正在考虑参与可能的TikTok收购。目前还不清楚软银是想在现有竞购上与另一家实体合作，还是想创造新的努力。但由于软银是一家日本公司，特朗普政府似乎不太可能会这么做。(杰西卡·E·莱辛/The Information)。
YouTube的创建者呼吁TikTok明星在流感大流行期间继续狂欢。TikTok没有像Instagram和YouTube这样的平台那样的评论文化，在这些平台上，点名道姓的游戏继续蓬勃发展。(朱莉娅·亚历山大/The Verge)。
Facebook正在加速推出美国以外的“新闻”(News)选项卡。该公司正在制定协议，向几个国家的出版商支付费用，让他们在标签中包含他们的内容。(Sara Fischer/Axios)。
Facebook更新了Messenger房间，使这一功能更容易发现，并允许人们进一步个性化他们的房间体验。这一变化是在Messenger上周从App Store的榜首略有下降之后发生的。(Sarah Perez/TechCrunch)
谷歌提拔哈利玛·德莱恩·普拉多(Halimah DeLaine Prado)为公司新的总法律顾问。这一消息发布之际，这家科技巨头正面临严格的反垄断审查。(凯尔·戴利/Axios)。
我们可能应该更小心地考虑我们给手机上的应用程序提供了多少数据访问。有些网站，比如谷歌地图，需要位置数据来提供帮助。但如果你只是使用TikTok滚动浏览其他人的视频，你不需要让这款应用程序访问你的相机和麦克风。文/布赖恩·X·陈(Brian X.Chen)/《纽约时报》。
今天在Zoom课堂上，我的教授不经意地提到了她的猫，一个勇敢的学生开口了：pic.twitter.com/C00eCYQydb。
-肖恩·布鲁斯特(@TheSeanBrewster)2020年8月25日
note_en:
Apple and Facebook are fighting again, and how you feel about it says something about who you trust to represent your interests in the strange tech landscape of 2020.
 Start with a rather academic question that may make your eyes glaze over in spite of yourself: When does pro-privacy regulation overstep its bounds to become anticompetitive? It’s a question  I found myself asking last year, when Apple canceled Facebook’s enterprise certificate temporarily following revelations that the company had been using those certificates to conduct market research. And it’s a question I’m thinking about today, as Apple intervenes to unilaterally reshape a market — in ways that once again put Facebook on the defensive.
 The issue at stake is a technical one, but it’s worth learning a little bit about — if only for the fact that, assuming you use an iPhone, it’s going to result in a lot of pop-ups on your phone once you upgrade your device in a month or so. The advertising industry assigns a unique code to each device called an Identification for Advertisers, or IDFA. Knowing your IDFA can help advertisers tell whether their ads are effective, particularly when they’ve shown you the same ad in multiple places. Facebook uses the IDFA as part of Audience Network, its ad network for developers.
 Starting with iOS 14, which will ship this fall, Apple will begin requiring that developers show you a warning that they are collecting your IDFA, and you’ll have to opt in to sharing it. Some large percentage of users can be expected to say “no thanks.”
 On Wednesday, Facebook said it would stop collecting IDFA altogether. Here’s  Kurt Wagner at Bloomberg:
 Facebook on Wednesday said it won’t collect IDFA through its own apps on iOS 14 devices, a decision that will “severely impact” Audience Network. Thousands of developers use the Facebook platform to fill the ad inventory within their mobile apps, and without IDFA information to help target those marketing messages, Audience Network revenue could drop as much as 50%, the company said. Facebook is considering eliminating the service altogether for iOS 14 users.
 “This is not a change we want to make, but unfortunately Apple’s updates to iOS 14 have forced this decision,” Facebook wrote in a blog post. “We know this may severely impact publishers’ ability to monetize through Audience Network on iOS 14, and, despite our best efforts, may render Audience Network so ineffective on iOS 14 that it may not make sense to offer it on iOS 14 in the future.”
 Facebook’s own ad business will be mostly fine, since your profile and activity on its apps contain data way more useful than a simple device ID. But the company says that a lot of its advertising clients will suffer.
 A common reaction to all this on Twitter today was:  boo-hoo. “Aww, poor baby, Facebook,” says my pal Joanna Stern,  in a representative tweet. “Now it has to ask permission to track everything you do on your phone.” Said the pseudonymous Internet of Shit: “ I like how Facebook is spinning this as a bad thing.”
 To be clear, Apple’s move here legitimately promotes privacy, if only at the margins. And it does so in the same way that Facebook often congratulates itself for doing: by putting the user in control. My friend Walt Mossberg picked up on this point: “In the forthcoming version of iOS, Apple is simply switching from an obscure opt-out system for apps that want to track users across the web to an explicit opt-in system,”  he wrote. “At last! In my view, we should have a law requiring explicit opt-ins for the collection of personal data.”
 But do you feel at all differently knowing that Apple targets its own advertising at you using a wide variety of data it collects from your phone?  Because it does! Here are  Patience Haggin and Jeff Horwitz in the  Wall Street Journal:
 Apple isn’t a big player in online advertising, but it does have its own small business that personalizes ads shown in the App Store and on Apple News based on where users go and what users do in Apple’s apps. The company is applying separate rules for its own ad-personalization; to opt out, users must find an option in the iPhone’s settings.
 Apple says it’s applying one standard everywhere. The IDFA restrictions apply only for developers who want to move data between companies; all developers can get access to the data generated from within their own apps. But this neatly sets aside the fact that Apple doesn’t have to move data between companies to run its ad business — because it owns the device, the operating system, the App Store, the data, and the advertising network.
 OK, but who really suffers here? We know Facebook can take the hit, and surely the big game publishers will find other ways to advertise their games. But  Recode reports that  big publishers are considering abandoning iOS altogether because the value of advertising is expected to decline so much:
 “It makes it much harder for us to monetize our Apple app users, who are an incredibly loyal readership. And quite frankly, it puts at risk our ability to provide an Apple app,” says Martin Clarke, publisher of DMG Media, which owns the UK’s Daily Mail and other publications; the company says its  MailOnline iOS app attracts 1.2 million users per day. “There’s no point in providing an app for a platform that monetizes less well than other platforms.”
 Such a steep decline has happened before. When Apple implemented similar anti-tracking features on its Safari web browser,  the cost of reaching customers there declined by 60 percent,  The Information reported last year. If you believe that free, ad-supported news is beneficial to a healthy democracy, it’s worth noting that all these pro-privacy changes come at a cost.
 I’m not in love with ads, I swear. I pay extra each month to turn them off on YouTube, Hulu, and even   The Verge. But it’s also true that ads subsidize a lot of important services. The less money you have, the more you rely on free services from companies like Facebook to live your daily life.
 There should absolutely be hard limits on the kinds of data advertisers can collect, what they can do with it, and how they can store it. There should arguably be  more limits on all of those things than there are today.
 But just as I tense up when I see Apple  dictating the revenue model of an email startup, or  threatening to withhold bug fixes from WordPress blog owners if its parent company doesn’t implement in-app purchasing, I worry when I see the extent to which one business can change the terms of a whole economy. Particularly when no one affected has any meaningful way to appeal.
 Few will cry any tears for Facebook over IFDA. I understand the impulse many have to view this particular dispute through the lens of the good team beating up the bad one. But the events of this summer have folks I know in Silicon Valley asking the same question over and over: who will Apple put the squeeze on next?
   Trending down:   Google employees knew the company’s location privacy settings were confusing and potentially misleading, according to newly unsealed documents from a consumer fraud lawsuit that the state of Arizona filed against the tech giant. The lawsuit came after the Associated Press published an article about how Google tracks users — even those that had turned tracking off. (Kate Cox /  Ars Technica)
  ⭐  Two protesters in Kenosha, Wisconsin were killed Tuesday night. The violence may be tied to a  Facebook event posted by a self-described militia, which referred to the event as a “call to arms.” Facebook has taken down the event, as well as the group’s Facebook Page. Here’s Russell Brandom at  The Verge:
 For three days, Kenosha has been racked with protests over the shooting of Jacob Blake, a 29-year-old father of six who was shot in the back by police. The protests have incurred significant property damage,  destroying a local Department of Corrections facility on Monday night.
 In a post Tuesday afternoon, the Kenosha Guard Facebook group encouraged an armed response to the ongoing unrest. “Any patriots willing to take up arms and defend our city tonight from the evil thugs?” the post reads. “No doubt they are currently planning on the next part of the city to burn tonight.”
 At 11:45PM Tuesday night,  two people were killed after a confrontation between protestors and men armed with long rifles, with a third sustaining injuries. Video of the shooting appears to show an armed militia member firing at protestors. The local sheriff’s office is still investigating the shooting, and the perpetrator has yet to be identified.
 At least two separate  Facebook users reported the Kenosha Guard account for inciting violence prior to the shooting. In each case, Facebook moderators examined the group and found that it didn’t violate the platform’s policies. The company took down the Kenosha Guard Facebook page after the shootings. (Russell Brandom /  The Verge)
 The young man charged with killing two protestors in Kenosha, Wisconsin was identified on social media as  Twitter and  YouTube users attempted to reconstruct the shootings. Kyle Rittenhouse was identified as a “fugitive from justice” and will be charged with first-degree intentional homicide. (Adi Robertson /  The Verge)
 Twitter suspended a number of accounts for sharing a viral message claiming to be a Black Lives Matter protester who previously voted Democrat but is now switching to Republican. The message was first shared by a seemingly inauthentic account, then copied and pasted by others. (Marianna Spring / BBC)
  QAnon conspiracy theorists are targeting evangelicals on  Facebook. Their message that  Donald Trump is fighting a secret Satanic pedophile ring run by liberal elites mirrors the “Satanic panic” of the 1980s and 1990s. (Abby Ohlheiser /  MIT Technology Review)
 More than 2,400 police departments have signed contracts with  Clearview AI, a controversial facial recognition firm. In a video interview with Jason Calacanis, the CEO of the company said “It’s an honor to be at the center of the debate now and talk about privacy.” An honor! (Elizabeth Lopatto /  The Verge)
 The UK government will likely pass restrictions on  TikTok, but will not block the company from setting up its international headquarters in London. But the rules will stop TikTok from moving user data out of the country. (Kitty Donaldson, Katharine Gemmell and Nate Lanxon / Bloomberg)
 A group of more than 2,000 online sellers filed an antitrust lawsuit against  Amazon in India. The suit alleges Amazon favors some vendors who sell goods at a discount and drive out independent retailers. (Aditya Kalra / Reuters)
  ⭐  The main administrator of Scottish  Wikipedia is an American who doesn’t know any Scots, and instead writes articles in strangely misspelled English. The situation highlights a major problem for the site — many language editions of Wikipedia only have a few editors, and those people have an outsized control over what ends up on the site. Edward Ongweso Jr. at  Vice describes the problem:
 For example, it was found a few years ago that an  admin of Croatian Wikipedia was a Holocaust denier, and was inserting those views into Croatian Wikipedia articles. There was little anyone could do to stop them. Because Wikipedia ranks so highly on Google and has become seen as a trusted, neutral source of information, it is often used by machine learning researchers as a corpus to train languages on, and by ordinary people as a first entry point into a topic.
 The Reddit post goes on to argue that the damage done by Amaryllis may be more severe than anyone anticipates. Before all of this had even started, there was apparently a poor conception of the Scots language that stemmed directly from the state of its Wikipedia. As a result, “this person has possibly done more damage to the Scots language than anyone else in history.” Because of their “cultural vandalism,” it’s possible that many people think Scots is a “horribly mangled rendering of English” rather than a language of its own.
 Microsoft planned to take a small stake in  TikTok and become one of the app’s minority investors. Then  President Trump got involved, and the quiet deal became an international soap opera. This story charts how it happened. (Mike Isaac and Andrew Ross Sorkin /  The New York Times)
 SoftBank is considering getting involved in a possible  TikTok acquisition. It’s unclear whether SoftBank wants to team up with another entity on an existing bid or create a new effort. But because SoftBank is a Japanese firm, it seems unlikely the Trump Administration would go for it. (Jessica E. Lessin /  The Information)
 YouTube creators are calling out  TikTok stars for continuing to party during the pandemic. TikTok doesn’t have the same type of commentary culture as platforms like  Instagram and YouTube, where the name-and-shame game continues to thrive. (Julia Alexander /  The Verge)
 Facebook is accelerating the launch of the News tab outside the US. The company is working out deals to pay publishers in several countries to include their content in the tab. (Sara Fischer /  Axios)
 Facebook updated Messenger Rooms to make the feature easier to discover and allow people to further personalize their Rooms experience. The change comes after Messenger dipped slightly from the top of the App Store last week. (Sarah Perez /  TechCrunch)
 Google promoted Halimah DeLaine Prado to be the company’s new general counsel. The news comes in a moment of intense antitrust scrutiny for the tech giant. (Kyle Daly /  Axios)
 We should probably be more careful about how much data access we give the apps on our phone. Some, like  Google Maps, need location data to be helpful. But if you’re only using  TikTok to scroll through other peoples’ videos, you don’t need to give the app access to your camera and microphone. (Brian X. Chen /  The New York Times)

— Sean Brewster (@TheSeanBrewster)  August 25, 2020

note:
2018年，美国发生了130多万起火灾，与房屋火灾有关的死亡人数中，近五分之三发生在没有烟雾报警器或功能不正常的房子里。
港湾公司刚刚完成了500万美元的种子期融资，希望让用户做好更充分的准备。
该产品将于10月份推出，旨在游戏化为灾难做日常准备的过程。使用NOAA、FEMA和USGS等机构提供的公开数据，以及地形图和建筑法规来确定单个家庭的风险，Harbor会查看用户的位置和他们家的一般状况，以确定该用户及其财产面临的风险类型。
从那时起，该平台每周都会列出一份清单，让用户随时做好准备，无论是跟踪手头的水量(针对飓风季节的人)，还是检查电池电量和烟雾报警器的功能。
首席执行官丹·凯斯勒(Dan Kessler)表示：“对我们来说，这不是买一个行李包。”“这是关于做你需要做好准备的事情。你的计划比你的包重要多了。你的包也很重要，但是没有规划就完全没有意义了。问题是，很多人，尤其是现在发生野火的人，都在说‘我没有行李包’，然后他们在亚马逊上花50美元买了一个。但他们在那一刻并没有像买包之前那样做好任何准备。“。
港湾不仅希望帮助用户为灾难做好准备，包括围绕准备设备进行精心策划的产品推荐，而且还帮助指导他们度过灾难本身和灾难的后果，根据具体情况提供循序渐进的指导。
尽管Harbor尚未公开发布该产品，但该公司已经准备好双重商业模式，其中包括电子商务和应用本身的免费增值订阅计划。
在这轮500万美元的融资中，唯一的投资者是25Madison，这是一家总部位于纽约的风险工作室，从一开始就为公司提供孵化和资金支持。25Madison在1月份聘请了前Headspace高管丹·凯斯勒(Dan Kessler)担任首席执行官。凯斯勒聘请爱德华多·丰塞卡(Eduardo Fonseca)担任首席技术官，后者曾担任GoodRx的CTO。
总体而言，港湾由一个10人的团队组成，该公司拒绝透露团队中任何关于多样性和包容性的情况，称“丹和团队感到非常自豪的是，女性和代表不足的群体的构成高于科技行业的平均水平，包括顾问委员会。”
该顾问委员会包括一些灾难领域的知名专家，包括联邦应急管理局(FEMA)前局长布罗克·朗(Brock Long)、现任外交关系委员会(Council On Foreign Relations)气候变化政策高级研究员爱丽丝·希尔(Alice Hill)、哈佛大学肯尼迪政府学院(Kennedy School Of Government)教授和CNN国家安全分析师(曾担任国土安全部助理部长)朱丽叶·卡耶姆(Juliette Kayyem)等。
note_en:
There were  more than 1.3 million fires in the United States in 2018, and  nearly three out of every five deaths related to a house fire happened in a house where there was no smoke alarm or it didn’t function properly.
 Harbor, a company that just closed on a $5 million seed round, wants to make users more prepared.
  The product, which will launch in October, aims to gamify the process of doing everyday preparation for disasters. Using publicly available data from agencies like NOAA, FEMA, and USGS, as well as land maps and building codes to pinpoint individual household risk,  Harbor   takes a look at the user’s location and the general state of their home to determine types of risks to that individual user and their property.
 From there, the platform curates a weekly checklist for the user to stay prepared, whether it’s keeping track of the amount of water on hand (for those in the path of hurricane season) or checking the battery levels and functionality of a smoke alarm.
 “For us, it’s not about buying a go bag,” said CEO Dan Kessler. “It’s about doing the things you need to be to be prepared. Your plan is a heck of a lot more important than your bag. Your bag is also important, but without the planning it’s completely pointless. The problem is a lot of people, especially right now with the wildfires happening are saying ‘I don’t have a go bag,’ and they buy one for $50 on Amazon. But they are not any more prepared at that moment as they were before they bought the bag.”
 Not only does harbor want to help users prepare for disasters, including curated product recommendations around preparedness equipment, but also helps guide them through the disaster itself and the aftermath, offering step by step instructions based on the specific situation.
 Though harbor hasn’t launched the product publicly, the company is prepared with a two-fold business model which includes ecommerce and a freemium subscription plan for the app itself.
 The sole investor in the $5 million round was  25madison, a NY-based venture studio that incubates and funds companies from inception. 25madison brought on Dan Kessler, a former Headspace executive, as CEO in January. Kessler brought on Eduardo Fonseca as Chief Technology Officer, who previously served as CTO of GoodRx.
 In total, harbor is made up of a team of ten employees, and the company declined to share any states around diversity and inclusion on the team, saying “Dan and the team are very proud that the makeup of women and underrepresented groups is above tech industry averages, including the advisory board.”
 The advisory board includes a number of notable experts in the disaster space, including former administrator of FEMA Brock Long, current senior fellow for climate change policy at the Council on Foreign relations Alice Hill, and professor at Harvard’s Kennedy School of Government and CNN national security analyst (who served as Assistant Secretary at the DHS) Juliette Kayyem, among others.
title: 应急准备平台Harbor获得500万美元种子资金

note:
NerdWallet表示，交易的财务条款没有披露。知道你的钱将成为NerdWallet在美国以外的第一个业务，并将在国际总经理梅根·泰德福德(Megan Tedford)的领导下，引领公司进一步的国际扩张努力。
这笔交易突显了这家总部位于旧金山的初创公司的悄然增长，目前该公司拥有1.6亿用户。它上一次筹集资金是在2015年-1亿美元(6900万美元的股权，其余的是信用票据)-估值约为5.2亿美元。自那以后，该公司没有更新这一数字，但一直处于盈利状态，目前没有筹集更多资金的计划。投资者包括IVP、RRE Ventures、iGlobe Partners和硅谷银行。
从那时起，NerdWallet也大幅扩张，目前年收入超过1.5亿美元。在更多背景下，NerdWallet直接与Credit Karma(拥有1亿用户，今年早些时候被Intuit以71亿美元收购)、Credit Sesame(去年估计其估值约为10亿美元)以及其他一些同时提供咨询和金融内容的市场以及成本比较服务进行竞争，以衡量各种金融产品不同报价的相对成本。
NerdWallet将Know Your Money描述为英国最大的为企业服务的比较网站。去年，它有大约500万消费者和120万家企业在使用其产品，包括寻找和开立银行账户，获得贷款和安排抵押贷款，以及购买保险。
“我们期待着与NerdWallet联手，在我们团队帮助消费者了解、评估和比较金融产品的出色工作的基础上再接再厉，”与约翰·埃尔莫尔(John Ellmore)共同创立“知道你的钱”(Know Your Money)的杰森·塔西(Jason Tassie)在一份声明中说。与NerdWallet合作将帮助我们加快现有的增长计划，扩大我们的内容库、工具和指南，为用户提供更多的金融决策支持。了解你的钱和NerdWallet的目标是完全一致的，即让人们能够做出更好、更知情的财务决策。“。
泰德福德说，寻找和与Know Your Money谈判的整个过程在大流行之前就开始了，但基本上是在Zoom上空进行的，旅行在今年2月几乎完全停止-这是一个奇怪的情况，但每个人都已经学会了忍受这种情况。
这场大流行可能没有刺激这笔交易，但突显了两家公司可能面临的机会，因为消费者越来越多地在网上进行财务生活，但也希望随着经济摇摇欲坠和陷入衰退，在财务上得到更好的控制。
“这场流行病造成了对再融资和投资等领域的金融指导和产品的需求激增-我们今年在这些领域看到了创纪录的网站访问量。NerdWallet联合创始人兼首席执行官Tim Chen在一份声明中表示，向英国扩张是朝着我们的愿景迈出了重要的一步，在这个世界里，每个消费者都能自信地做出财务决定。消费者正在寻求更高水平的帮助，有了了解你的钱，我们希望在那里为尽可能多的人提供指导，跨越尽可能多的主题，在尽可能多的地方。我知道你的钱在帮助消费者找到和比较金融产品方面做了出色的工作，我们期待着通过这一伙伴关系加快这项工作。“
note_en:
Financial terms of the deal are not being disclosed, Nerdwallet said. Know Your Money will become Nerdwallet’s first operation outside of the US and will spearhead the company’s efforts for further international expansion under international general manager Megan Tedford.
 The deal underscores the quiet growth of the San Francisco-based startup, which now has 160 million users. It last raised money in 2015 — $100 million ($69 million in equity, and the rest in a credit note) — at a valuation of about $520 million. It hasn’t updated that number since, but has been profitable and has no plans to raise more funding for the moment. Investors include IVP, RRE Ventures, iGlobe Partners and Silicon Valley Bank.
  Nerdwallet has also expanded significantly since that time, and currently makes more than $150 million annually in revenues. For some more context, Nerdwallet competes directly with companies like Credit Karma (which has 100 million users and was  acquired by Intuit earlier this year for $7.1 billion), Credit Sesame (which last year  estimated that it’s valued at around $1 billion), along with a number of other marketplaces that both provide advice and financial content, as well as cost comparison services to weigh up the relative costs of different offers for various financial products.
 Nerdwallet describes Know Your Money as the UK’s largest comparison site serving businesses. It had some 5 million consumers and 1.2 million businesses using its products last year, which include looking for and opening bank accounts, getting loans and arranging mortgages, and getting insurance.
 “We’re looking forward to joining forces with  NerdWallet and building on the fantastic work our team has done helping consumers learn about, evaluate and compare financial products,” said Jason Tassie, who co-founded Know Your Money with John Ellmore, in a statement. “Working with  NerdWallet will help us accelerate our existing growth plans, expanding our content library, tools and guides to offer users more support in financial decision making. Know Your Money and  NerdWallet are perfectly aligned in their goal of empowering people to make better, more-informed financial decisions.”
 Tedford said that the whole process of finding and negotiating with Know Your Money started ahead of the pandemic but was essentially carried out over Zoom with travel all but completely halted in February of this year — a strange circumstance but one everyone has learned to live with.
 That pandemic may not have spurred this deal but has underscored where the opportunity might be for both companies, as consumers are increasingly carrying out more of their financial lives online but also hoping to be more fiscally in control as economies totter and fall into recession.
 “The pandemic has created a surge in demand for financial guidance and products in areas like refinance and investing — we’ve seen record visits to our site in these areas this year. Expansion to the UK is an important step towards our vision of a world where every consumer makes financial decisions with confidence,” said Tim Chen, Co-Founder and CEO of  NerdWallet, in a statement. “Consumers are looking for a greater level of help, and with Know Your Money, we want to be there providing the guidance to as many people, across as many topics and in as many places as possible. Know Your Money has done a fantastic job helping consumers find and compare financial products and we’re looking forward to accelerating that work through this partnership.”
title: NerdWallet在美国以外扩张时收购英国的Know Your Money
title_en: Nerdwallet acquires UK’s Know Your Money as it expands outside the US

note:
Axios从多个知情人士处了解到，微软正在与沃尔玛合作，试图从中国的ByteDance手中收购TikTok的美国业务。
现状：这个想法将有助于将TikTok美国变成更多面向创建者和用户的电子商务应用，就像TikTok母公司ByteDance在中国推出类似应用所做的那样。
就在几个小时前，迪士尼前高管凯文·梅耶尔辞去了TikTok首席执行官和ByteDance首席运营官一职，有消息称，他已被ByteDance排除在收购谈判之外。
微软和ByteDance之间还没有签署协议，从技术上讲，双方在9月1日之前已经签署了协议。15日向白宫提交交易提案供批准。
人们的讨论仍然只集中在TikTok在美国、加拿大、澳大利亚和新西兰的业务上。
言外之意：沃尔玛也有可能与甲骨文进行了谈判，甲骨文也在继续与字节跳动进行谈判。
软银集团(SoftBank Group)没有参与微软/沃尔玛的努力，尽管据消息人士透露，这家日本公司已经向ByteDance进行了询问。
一句话：沃尔玛为微软的努力增添了更多的资金火力和电子商务技术。但沃尔玛在中国也有很大的影响力，这可能会使微软和字节跳动(ByteDance)在特朗普政府通过交易的努力复杂化。
note_en:
Microsoft is working with Walmart on its efforts to buy TikTok's U.S. business from China's ByteDance, Axios has learned from multiple sources close to the process.
 The state of play: The idea would be to help  turn TikTok U.S. into more of an e-commerce app for creators and users, much like what TikTok parent company ByteDance does with a similar app in China.
 The development comes just hours after former Disney executive Kevin Mayer  resigned as CEO of TikTok and COO of ByteDance, with sources saying he'd been excluded by ByteDance from the takeover talks.
 There is not yet a signed agreement between Microsoft and ByteDance, as the sides technically have until Sept. 15 to submit a deal proposal to the White House for approval.
 Conversations continue to only center on TikTok's operations in the U.S., Canada, Australia, and New Zealand.
 Between the lines: It's possible that Walmart also has had discussions with Oracle, which also continues to negotiate with ByteDance.
 SoftBank Group is not involved in the Microsoft/Walmart effort, although the Japanese firm has made inquiries to ByteDance, per sources.
 The bottom line: Walmart adds more financial firepower and e-commerce know-how to Microsoft's efforts. But Walmart also has a significant presence in China, which could complicate Microsoft and ByteDance's efforts to get a deal through the Trump administration.
title: 消息人士称，沃尔玛表示正在与微软就TikTok交易展开合作，目的是将TikTok美国变成面向创建者和用户的电子商务应用
title_en: Walmart says it is working with Microsoft on the TikTok deal, sources say with the idea of turning TikTok US into an e-commerce app for creators and users

note:
世界上最大的加密货币欺诈案之一的分裂始于一种嫉妒行为。
Ruja Ignatova的哥哥后来在联邦法院作证说，Ruja Ignatova的OneCoin赚了数十亿美元，她怀疑男友在2017年底欺骗了她。因此，他说，她让一名同事在男友佛罗里达州的公寓下面租了一套公寓，然后钻穿地板进行窃听。
她发现的是另一种背叛：他正在与联邦调查局(FBI)合作。
几周后，被粉丝称为加密女王的伊格纳托娃消失了。纽约的联邦检察官指控她犯有欺诈罪。
OneCoin的故事甚至在加密货币时代的奇葩中也脱颖而出。司法部表示，OneCoin的账户显示，从2014年第四季度到2016年第三季度，OneCoin的收入约为40亿美元，吸引了从非洲到美国、委内瑞拉到中国的投资者。
note_en:
The splintering of one of the world’s biggest cryptocurrency frauds began with an act of jealousy.
  Ruja Ignatova, whose OneCoin raked in billions, suspected her boyfriend of stringing her along in late 2017, her brother later testified in federal court. So, he said, she had an associate rent an apartment beneath the boyfriend’s Florida digs and drill through the floor to eavesdrop.
  What she discovered was another kind of betrayal: that he was cooperating with the Federal Bureau of Investigation.
  Weeks later Ms. Ignatova, known among her fans as the Cryptoqueen, disappeared. Federal prosecutors in New York have charged her with fraud.
  The story of OneCoin stands out even among the outlandish capers of the cryptocurrency era. The Justice Department said OneCoin accounts show it took in some $4 billion from the fourth quarter of 2014 to the third quarter of 2016, sucking in investors from Africa to the U.S., Venezuela to China.
title: OneCoin获得了数十亿美元的收入。然后它的头目就消失了
title_en: OneCoin Took in Billions. Then Its Leader Vanished
